title: Tight relation between the physical effects of a quantum measurement and the information gained about an observable
abstract: The dynamics of quantum measurements defines a precise relation between the information gained about one physical property of a system and the observable changes in another physical property of the same system. Here, we express this relation in terms of the Hilbert space superpositions of the corresponding eigenstates and show how the probability of an observable physical change can be obtained from the Bayesian update of the probabilities associated with the information obtained in the measurement. Our analysis demonstrates that the superposition principle provides the tightest possible expression of the trade-off between information and back action in a quantum measurement.
summary: This paper develops a formal theory connecting information acquisition during quantum measurements to the physical disturbance experienced by the system. It derives that the probability of observable change (back action) is mathematically equivalent to $1$ minus the total update fidelity measure derived from the measured outcomes. This provides a tight, quantitative link between knowledge and physical effect in quantum measurement theory.
main_problem: The paper establishes a precise, quantitative relationship between the information gained about one quantum observable ($\hat{A}$) during measurement and the resulting physical change (back action) on another observable ($\hat{B}$). It aims to resolve the conceptual difficulty of separating statistical knowledge updates from genuine dynamical state changes.
main_result:
The probability of physical back action ($P(	ext{bout} 
eq \mathbf{b})$) is shown to be fundamentally linked to, and in some cases equal to, $1$ minus the total measurement update fidelity ($1 - F_{AM}$).
method: The analysis employs Hilbert space formalism, utilizing Bayesian updates for information gain and Kraus operators/resolution matrices to quantify physical state evolution (back action).
model_or_system: The study analyzes general quantum systems undergoing projective measurements of two observables ($\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$) starting from a pure input state $|\mathbf{b}angle$. The formalism is generalized to mixed states via purification.
key_observables:
Back action probability ($P(	ext{bout} 
eq \mathbf{b})$), Update Fidelity ($F_{AM}$), Mutual Information ($I(A, M)$), and Intrinsic Robustness ($I_A(\mathbf{b})$).
important_parameters: The relationship is governed by the structure of the resolution matrix $R(a, a')$, which quantifies correlations between measurement outcomes.
main_assumptions: The analysis carefully distinguishes between statistical information updates (Bayesian) and physical state changes (back action), requiring careful handling within the Hilbert space framework.
paper_structure:
The paper builds from defining the measurement process, deriving expressions for back action using Kraus operators, establishing identities linking back action to statistical fidelity measures ($P(	ext{bout} 
eq b) = 1 - F_{AM}$), and finally quantifying this relationship using resolution matrices.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding the fundamental trade-off between measurement precision (information gain) and disturbance (back action) in quantum mechanics, which is central to quantum measurement theory and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
